Senate Avenue • Indianapolis, IN 46204 (800) 451-6027 • (317) 232-8603 • Fax (317) 233-6647 • www.idem.IN.gov Mike Braun Clint Woods Governor Commissioner February 17, 2026 The Honorable Lee M. Zeldin Administrator U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Ave. NW, 1101A Washington, D.C. 20590 Submitted Electronically via Regulations.gov Re: Comments on Proposed Rulemaking “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” by the Indiana Department of Environmental Management, Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, FRL-6976.2-01-OW Dear Administrator Zeldin: On January 15, 2026, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) published in the Federal Register the proposed rule “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” (91 Fed. Reg. 2008), to update and clarify several substantive and procedural requirements for water quality certification under the Clean Water Act (CWA). The Indiana Department of Environmental Management (IDEM), as the state agency responsible for the daily implementation of the CWA water quality programs and the certifying authority for Section 401 water quality certifications in Indiana, is pleased to provide these comments in support of the proposed rule. The proposed rule offers common sense updates and a significant course correction imposed under the final rule “Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule” issued on September 27, 2023, at 88 Fed. Reg. 66558 (the “2023 Rule”). Most notably, the proposed rule would revise several procedural and substantive aspects of the 2023 Rule. IDEM previously provided feedback on EPA’s July 7, 2025, request for input on implementation challenges or regulatory uncertainty associated with the 2023 Rule at 90 Fed. Reg. 29828. As noted in that feedback, IDEM has effectively implemented the 2023 Rule, but noted areas where additional clarity may be appropriate. IDEM supports the revision and amendment to § 121.3, the scope of certification, and § 121.7, certification decisions. With respect to the revision made to § 121.3, IDEM agrees that “[t]he plain language of CWA section 401(a) directs states to certify that any discharge resulting from the proposed Federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with the enumerated provisions of the CWA.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2023. As EPA points out, this interpretation is further supported by the revisions made in 1972 to CWA section 401, changing “activity” to “discharge.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2024-25. As such, IDEM agrees that EPA’s interpretation is the best reading of Section 401. See Loper Bright v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 400 (2024). Additionally, IDEM is supportive of EPA requiring certifying authorities to include specific contents in all certification decisions with the amendments to § 121.7. Currently, IDEM’s certification conditions are within the appropriate scope, the language of which was successfully coordinated with Indiana’s three Corps Districts. The language used to support each condition includes legal citations to support conditions that are included to protect water quality. IDEM seeks clarity on a scenario in which our federal partners were to disagree and exclude a condition. Regarding the proposed changes to the Section 401(a)(2) process, IDEM is supportive of the proposed changes and is confident in its ability to comply. For coordination with neighboring jurisdictions, IDEM has previously successfully worked with Kentucky and reviewed and supported the conditions Kentucky required on projects proposing discharge of dredge and fill to the Ohio River. So far, all the projects, when permitted with the conditions in Kentucky’s 401 water quality certifications, comply with Indiana’s water quality standards. IDEM appreciates the opportunity to review these projects and believes the requirement to coordinate is not overly burdensome and will help streamline certifications. EPA has requested comment on the proposed contents of a request for certification under § 121.5. IDEM believes the proposed rule would help provide clear and specific guidance on what is needed to fulfill the proposed certification request. IDEM appreciates the opportunity to comment on EPA’s proposed rule “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations.” IDEM supports EPA’s underlying rationale for reversing the 2023 Rule’s “activity as a whole” approach because Loper Bright requires that agencies adopt the “best reading” of the statute, rather than merely a “reasonable” reading under the Chevron deference doctrine. 91 Fed. Reg. at 2023. IDEM agrees with EPA that the 2023 Rule interpretation “does not reflect the best reading of the statute.” Id. In short, Loper Bright compels EPA to reevaluate whether its interpretation is the “best reading” of the statute, and the textual arguments set forth by EPA in the proposed rule support a discharge-focused approach. IDEM does not anticipate the revisions in the proposed rule to detract from its ability to maintain compliance. IDEM looks forward to continued collaboration on CWA implementation issues with EPA. Sincerely, Clint Woods Commissioner Indiana Department of Environmental Management
